1.description: Volatility of USD-Korean won exchange rate The annualized 30-day historical volatility of the USD-KRW exchange rate.

2.description: Gross foreign capital inflows Korea’s gross foreign capital inflows. The vertical line represents the imposition of the regulation.

3.description: FX positions of exporters and banks before the regulation The left panel illustrates the structure of the exporter FX position and the right panel illustrates the structure of the bank FX position prior to the regulation. Exporters had a long position in foreign currency (because of export sales) and hedged the exposure by taking short positions in FXD. As banks are firms’ FXD counterparties, banks had a long position in foreign currency due to the FXD. Banks hedged the long exposure by foreign currency borrowing.

4.description: FX forward hedging demand and bank positions covering The left panel shows the amount of USD forwards each nonbank sector purchased from or sold to banks during the first three quarters of 2007. The right panel shows how banks covered the net long position of US$37 billion in forwards. Source: Bank of Korea (2008).

5.description: FXD position to capital ratio, before and after the regulation (foreign banks) The histogram of the FXD position to the capital (DPTC) ratio of foreign banks, 6 months before and 6 months after the first announcement of regulation. The vertical line represents the regulatory cap on the DPTC ratio.

6.description: FXD position to capital ratio, before and after the regulation (domestic banks) The histogram of the FXD position to the capital (DPTC) ratio of domestic banks, 6 months before and 6 months after the first announcement of regulation. The vertical line represents the regulatory cap on the DPTC ratio.

7.description: FXD position by treatment The top panel plots the aggregate FXD position. The vertical solid (dotted) lines indicate the announcement (effective) dates of changes in the minimum FXD capital requirement, with red (gray) lines indicating tightening (loosening) adjustments. The middle panel plots the normalized average FXD position of constrained (solid) and unconstrained (dotted) banks. The average FXD positions are normalized such that they equal one at the imposition of the regulation. The bottom panel plots the minimum FXD capital requirements. The blue line represents the simple average, and the red line represents the weighted average minimum FXD capital requirements where the weight is FXD position.

8.description: Coefficient by month Plot of λt over time in the following specification: Yit=β0+β1Constrainedi+β2Postt+∑tλtConstrainedi×γt+εit, where Post takes the value of one for the time period after the regulation (June 2010) and zero otherwise. γt is the time dummy variable for each month. The vertical lines represent the adjustments of the regulatory cap. The first three vertical lines (in red) represent tightening adjustments, and the last vertical line (in gray) represents a loosening adjustment.

A.1.description: Number of countries using macroprudential FX regulations Source: IMF-integrated Macroprudential Policy Database.

A.2.description: CIP bases Ten-day moving average of daily CIP bases for different maturities. CIP basis at time t for maturity n is defined in Equation (12).

A.3.description: Korean won exchange rate The exchange rate is defined as the value of US$1 in Korean won (KRW). A higher exchange rate indicates depreciation of the KRW.

A.4.description: Correlations between firm characteristics and firm exposure Binned scatterplots of firm characteristics (export share, profitability, FC liability share, and firm size) and firm exposure to the regulation.

A.5.description: CIP deviations: Short term and long term Ten-day moving average of 3-year (solid) and 3-month (dotted) USD-KRW CIP deviations, where CIP deviation is defined in Equation (12). The first three red vertical lines represent tightening adjustments, and the last gray vertical line represents a loosening adjustment.

C.1.description: Optimal investment given internal fund k = 0.1 was used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.2.description: Firm value given internal fund k = 0.1 was used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.3.description: Export quantity and hedging quantity as a function of hedging friction k = 0.1 and w0=25,000 were used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.4.description: Expected firm value as a function of hedging friction k = 0.1 and w0=25,000 were used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.5.description: Hedging quantity as a function of external financing friction k = 0.1 and w0=25,000 were used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.6.description: Reduction in exports: High k versus low k firms kH=0.1,\kL=0.05⁠, and w0=25,000 were used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.7.description: Borrowing versus hedging friction kH=0.1,\kL=0.05⁠, and w0H=25,000 are used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.8.description: Reduction in exports: Interaction of w0 and k. High k = 0.1, low k = 0.05, high w0=25,000⁠, and low w0=0 were used. Table C.1 reports the other parameter values.

C.9.description: Numerical value of ∂3q∂h∂k∂wo over a range of k, w0, and h values

C.10.description: Absolute risk aversion versus internal fund (⁠γC=1.1⁠)

C.11.description: Absolute risk aversion versus internal fund and external financing friction (⁠γC=1.1⁠)

C.12.description: Absolute risk aversion versus internal fund (⁠γC=2⁠)
